content:
7 min read
3 hours ago

--

I wanted one script that drafts emails in my voice. I ended up building a router so Groq’s 429s wouldn’t ruin my Tuesday.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Setup
I have this small Python script. Nothing clever — OpenAI SDK, a prompt, done. I paste in an email I’ve actually sent before, tell it what the new message should be about, and it spits out a draft that sounds like me. Not “professional assistant” me. Me me.
It worked great for about a week.
Then Groq started returning 429 in the middle of a two-line email. Gemini’s quota had reset on a schedule I still don’t fully understand. OpenAI was fine, but burning paid credits on “hey, I’ll be at standup” felt wrong. So I did what every developer does: I started commenting and uncommenting base_url lines in .env, juggling keys, rerunning the script, sighing.
At some point I realized I wasn’t building an email tool anymore. I was babysitting infrastructure for a 40-line script.
I didn’t want OpenRouter’s markup. I didn’t want to keep a LiteLLM container alive on a VPS just to forward HTTP. I wanted one URL my apps could trust, one client key per project, and routing rules I could change without redeploying Python.
So I built LLM Router — an OpenAI-compatible gateway on Cloudflare Workers. Provider fallback, key rotation, cooldowns when things break, per-app quotas, admin UI. D1 for config, KV for rate-limit state. Runtime deps are basically hono and react. That’s it.
🤔 Why Not Just Use What’s Out There?
Fair question. I looked.
OpenRouter is genuinely good if you want one key and a huge model catalog. But you’re renting their routing brain. You don’t get to say “for this app, try my spare Groq key first, then my Gemini key, then bail to OpenAI.” You’re also paying a cut on every call.
LiteLLM can do most of what I wanted. It’s also a thing you have to run. Python process, TLS, updates, “is the proxy up?” checks. For a side-project email script? Felt like buying a forklift to carry groceries.
Calling providers directly works until it doesn’t. One key, one model, no fallback, no per-app limits, no log of what failed. Fine for a demo. Annoying for something you actually use.
Cloudflare’s AI Gateway is solid for observability in front of a provider. I wanted more than that — multiple keys per provider, cooldowns after 429s, virtual route names like smart and fast, and an admin UI where I can reshuffle fallbacks on a whim.
What I landed on is smaller than LiteLLM, more hands-on than OpenRouter, and runs on Workers so I’m not SSH-ing into anything at midnight. You own the Worker, the D1 database, the routing table. Provider keys encrypted at rest. Client keys stored as hashes. No middleman.
If you already hoard free-tier API keys like I do, it’s basically a control panel for the chaos.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

🏗️ What Actually Happens on a Request
My email script calls model: "smart". It does not know what smart means. That’s intentional
Python OpenAI SDK
→ your Worker /v1/chat/completions
→ verify sk-router-... client key
→ look up route "smart" in D1
→ maybe reorder providers by recent health
→ pick a provider key (round-robin by last use)
→ call upstream
→ 429 or 5xx? cooldown in KV, try the next one
→ return response, log usage in the backgroundUnder the hood: Worker handles routing and auth. D1 holds providers, encrypted keys, routes, hashed client keys, usage logs. KV tracks cooldowns and RPM counters. React admin UI for the stuff you’d otherwise edit in JSON at 11pm.
Your apps only ever see https://llm-router.your-subdomain.workers.dev/v1 and a generated sk-router-... key. They never touch Groq/Gemini/OpenAI secrets.
🎯 The Script That Started It
This isn’t in the GitHub repo — I kept it local. Copy from here into any folder.
Paste one of your own sent emails as the tone sample. Tell it the topic. The router picks who answers; the script doesn’t care.
# email_writer.py — tone-matching email drafts via your LLM routerimport os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI
load_dotenv()
def create_client() -> OpenAI:
api_key = os.getenv("ROUTER_API_KEY") or os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")
if not api_key:
raise ValueError(
"API key not set. Add ROUTER_API_KEY to .env "
"or set OPENAI_API_KEY in the environment."
)
base_url = os.getenv(
"ROUTER_BASE_URL",
"https://llm-router.your-subdomain.workers.dev"
).rstrip("/")
if not base_url.endswith("/v1"):
base_url = f"{base_url}/v1"
return OpenAI(api_key=api_key, base_url=base_url)
def write_email(client: OpenAI, tone_sample: str, message: str) -> str:
"""Write an email matching tone_sample's tone and style about message."""
route = os.getenv("ROUTER_MODEL", "default") # e.g. "smart" or "fast"
prompt = (
"Write an email that matches the tone and style of this sample:\n\n"
f"{tone_sample}\n\n"
"Write the new email about:\n"
f"{message}"
)
response = client.chat.completions.create(
model=route,
messages=[
{
"role": "system",
"content": (
"You are a helpful assistant that writes emails in the user's "
"tone and style. Return only the email body."
),
},
{"role": "user", "content": prompt},
],
)
content = response.choices[0].message.content
if not content:
raise RuntimeError("Model returned an empty response")
return content
if __name__ == "__main__":
client = create_client()
# A real email you sent before — not a made-up persona.
sample = (
"Hey team,\n\n"
"Just a quick heads-up — I'll be out tomorrow afternoon. "
"Ping me if anything urgent comes up.\n\n"
"Thanks"
)
print(write_email(client, sample, "Confirm attendance for Friday's standup."))
Ran that against my smart route last week. Got something like:
Hey team,Quick note — I'll be at Friday's standup. Let me know if the time shifts.
Thanks
Close enough that I only tweaked one line.
The nice bit: the Python file says model="smart". In the admin UI, smart might be Gemini first, then Groq, then a cheap OpenAI model. Shuffled that order on a whim Thursday. Didn’t touch the script.
That’s the thing I actually wanted — apps speak in intentions (smart, fast, default), not in gemini-2.5-flash on key #3.
🧠 The Boring Parts That Matter
A few details that saved me headaches:
Virtual routes. model in the request is a route name. Unknown name → falls back to default. Your client code stays stable while you experiment with providers.
Cooldowns. Hit a 429? Key goes on timeout in KV. Router tries the next key or provider. You find out from the usage log, not from a broken script.
Multiple keys per provider. Yes, you can add two Groq keys. Oldest last_used_at gets picked. Sounds minor. Matters when you’re squeezing free tiers.
Quotas per client key. Optional RPM and daily token caps. Handy when you hand a key to a side project and don’t want it eating your whole Groq budget by lunch.
Adaptive ordering (optional). If enabled, providers with better recent success rates float up. Based on D1 usage over a window you configure. I leave it on.
502 with an attempts array. When everything fails, at least you know what failed instead of staring at a generic error.
Oh — streaming passes through without buffering. Usage logging runs in waitUntil() so the response isn’t waiting on bookkeeping. Small thing, easy to get wrong.
🎨 The Admin UI
Plain React, plain CSS. No Tailwind, no component library. I wanted the Worker stack to stay thin.
You add provider keys (write-only after save), sync models from OpenRouter, build fallback chains, mint client keys with optional limits, skim usage logs. Admin token lives in session storage — close the tab, it’s gone.
Not pretty enough for Dribbble. Functional enough that I haven’t edited wrangler.jsonc to change a route in weeks.
🚧 Stuff That Bit Me
Auth failures looked like my app was broken. Revoked provider key → 401. Router now treats that as “bad key, next” instead of surfacing upstream’s error to the client.
workers.dev subdomain is per-account, not per-Worker. First deploy tripped me up. CI uses WORKERS_DEV_SUBDOMAIN now. Documented in the README because future-me is forgetful.
Free tiers are a routing strategy. My fast route is Groq. smart leans Gemini, then OpenAI. default is whatever I’m trying to conserve that week. The router makes that a config change, not a code change.
🛠️ Try It
Node 18+, free Cloudflare account.
git clone https://github.com/ChandreshRao/llm-router.git
cd llm-router
npm install
npm run db:migrate:local# .dev.vars for local:
# ADMIN_TOKEN=sk-admin-change-me
# ENCRYPTION_KEY=use-a-long-random-string-here
npm run build
npx wrangler dev
Open the local URL, log in with ADMIN_TOKEN, add a provider key, wire up a route, generate a client key.
Deploy:
npx wrangler login
npm run deploy
npx wrangler secret put ADMIN_TOKEN
npx wrangler secret put ENCRYPTION_KEY
npm run db:migrateSet production secrets:
npx wrangler secret put ADMIN_TOKEN
npx wrangler secret put ENCRYPTION_KEY
npm run db:migrateEmail demo — any empty folder::
mkdir email-demo
cd email-demo# Paste the script above into email_writer.py, then:
@"
openai>=1.0.0
python-dotenv>=1.0.0
"@ | Out-File -Encoding utf8 requirements.txt
@"
ROUTER_API_KEY=sk-router-your-generated-client-key
ROUTER_BASE_URL=https://llm-router.your-subdomain.workers.dev
ROUTER_MODEL=smart
"@ | Out-File -Encoding utf8 .env
python -m venv .venv
.\.venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1
pip install -r requirements.txt
python email_writer.py
macOS/Linux: source .venv/bin/activate instead of the PowerShell line.
Full deploy guide — GitHub Actions, D1/KV IDs, troubleshooting — is in the README.
💡 Where It Goes From Here
Honestly? I’m still using it mostly for scripts like the email one. On the list: better cost hints in the UI, a webhook when a client key hits its daily cap, maybe a Home Assistant recipe because every LLM project eventually ends up in someone’s automation stack.
Wrapping Up
This started because I got tired of my email script dying when Groq had a mood swing. It turned into a Worker I deploy once and poke through a browser when I want to swap providers.
If you’ve got OpenAI-shaped clients already and a pile of API keys from free tiers, you probably don’t need another SaaS gateway. You need one stable URL and routing that fails over without you noticing.
Fork it, deploy to your own workers.dev subdomain, point your existing clients at it.
GitHub: github.com/ChandreshRao/llm-router
